- Researchers developing standardized perceptual learning interventions for clinical visual deficits
- Clinicians interested in portable, accessible tools for visual rehabilitation
- Investigators studying the role of attention in perceptual plasticity
- Teams conducting multi-site visual psychophysics studies

title: Nature immersion in virtual reality does not reduce naturally occurring muscle pain or improve motor outputs during prescribed or self-regulated tasks
authors: [Callum O'Malley, David Harris, Sam William Hughes, Jack Cottrell, Dan Lloyd, Sam Vine, Tom Arthur]
summary:
This preprint reports two within-participant crossover studies examining whether immersive virtual reality (VR) nature scenes can reduce naturally occurring muscle pain (NOMP) during physical exercise. While VR has established hypoalgesic effects for experimental and clinical pain, no prior study had tested whether these effects extend to the muscle pain that naturally accompanies physical tasks. Study 1 enrolled 12 healthy participants (mean age 20.8 years, 50% female) who completed a stepped incremental cycling task to exhaustion under three conditions: immersive VR (360-degree nature scene via Meta Quest 2), a 2D sham display of the same scene, and a no-intervention control. Study 2 enrolled 24 healthy participants (mean age 27.0 years, 50% female) who performed intermittent isometric handgrip contractions at fixed light (13/100) and strong (50/100) perceived effort intensities under the same three conditions.

In Study 1, VR did not significantly delay pain onset (F = 2.00, p = .159, eta-p-squared = 0.154) or prolong time to exhaustion (F = 1.71, p = .204, eta-p-squared = 0.135) compared to sham or control. A significant main effect for pain ratings emerged at 25% iso-time (F = 4.52, p = .023, eta-p-squared = 0.291), but Bonferroni-corrected pairwise comparisons were non-significant (VR vs. sham: d = -1.005, p = .055; VR vs. control: d = -0.641, p = .158). Pain ratings did not differ at 50%, 75%, or 100% iso-time. Perceived effort, motivation, and heart rate were similar across conditions. In Study 2, peak force (F = 0.50, p = .612), mean force (F = 0.71, p = .497), force-time integral (F = 1.15, p = .325), and NOMP ratings (F = 0.21, p = .815) were nearly identical across all three conditions. Peak RFD trended higher in the VR condition but did not reach significance (F = 2.89, p = .066). No intensity x condition interactions emerged for any force measures, and exploratory regression analyses showed no moderating effects of sex, environmental presence, or fatigue on the VR-NOMP relationship.
clinical_insight: These findings challenge the assumption that VR-based distraction generalizes across pain types. The authors propose that VR hypoalgesia is pain-type and potentially pain-intensity dependent: VR targets central processing mechanisms (reducing activity in the anterior cingulate cortex, insula, and thalamus), whereas NOMP is driven primarily by strong peripheral nociceptive signaling from metabo- and mechano-sensitive receptors in active musculature. This mismatch between central distraction and peripheral drive likely explains the null findings. For clinicians considering VR for pain management, the results suggest that nature-based immersive VR is unlikely to be effective for exercise-induced muscle pain, and its established benefits should be understood as specific to lower-intensity or non-musculoskeletal pain contexts. The small sample sizes (N=12 in Study 1, N=24 in Study 2) and subsequent reduced statistical power should be noted when interpreting these null results.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians and researchers exploring VR-based pain management interventions
- Sports medicine practitioners interested in non-pharmacological pain modulation
- Pain researchers investigating boundary conditions of distraction-based analgesia
- Rehabilitation specialists using VR in exercise-based recovery programs

title: Running ahead of the evidence? Rethinking the exercise-cognition consensus after five decades of research
authors: [Daniel Sanabria, Luis F. Ciria, Darías Holgado, Frantisek Bartos, Antonio Luque-Casado, Miguel Fernandez-del-Olmo, Pandelis Perakakis, Rafael Roman-Caballero]
summary:
This commentary critically examines the widely accepted claim that regular physical exercise improves cognitive function, arguing that the existing evidence base does not support the strength of certainty conveyed in public health messaging, WHO endorsements, and media coverage. The authors, several of whom authored the influential Ciria et al. (2023) Nature Human Behaviour umbrella review, present four main lines of argument. First, they highlight that a recent reanalysis of 2,239 effect size estimates (Bartos et al., 2025) found that between-study heterogeneity and publication bias render statements about average exercise-cognition effects meaningless, with the evidence compatible with both beneficial and null effects across most subgroups. Second, they document how their own critical umbrella review has been frequently miscited: of 114 Scopus citations tracked as of April 2026, 37% were miscitations that falsely claimed positive effects, while their corrective reply received only 8 citations compared to 30 for a critical commentary.

Third, the authors identify four conceptual pitfalls in mechanistic accounts: (a) reverse inference from brain changes (e.g., hippocampal volume increases do not uniquely indicate memory improvement, as this structure supports navigation, scene construction, prospection, and affective processing); (b) the flawed "brain as a muscle" analogy, given that far transfer is the exception rather than the rule in training research; (c) neural degeneracy, meaning the same cognitive outcome can emerge from multiple non-overlapping neural configurations, undermining single-marker inferences; and (d) the conflation of exercise-specific mechanisms with broader behavior-change confounds such as improved sleep, diet, self-efficacy, and social interaction. Fourth, they argue that the field has not meaningfully progressed methodologically in nearly 50 years since the first exercise-cognition RCT, continuing to produce underpowered studies with inadequate control conditions. They call for a paradigm shift toward preregistered reports, multilab collaborations, longitudinal designs, and novel hypothesis testing, while emphasizing that exercise's well-established benefits for physical health remain undisputed.
clinical_insight: For clinicians, this commentary serves as an important caution against overstating cognitive benefits when recommending exercise to patients. While the physical health benefits of regular exercise are unequivocal and should continue to be recommended, the evidence for specific cognitive improvements is far weaker than commonly represented. Clinicians should be aware that the 2020 WHO guidelines themselves rated most exercise-cognition RCTs as low or very low certainty of evidence. The authors note that exercise interventions may function as broad behavior-change programs (improving sleep, diet, social engagement, self-efficacy) rather than exerting direct, narrow cognitive effects through mechanisms like neurotrophic factors or angiogenesis. This reframing has practical implications: if cognitive benefits exist, they may derive from holistic lifestyle enrichment rather than physiological exertion per se, which could inform how exercise programs are structured and marketed in clinical and community settings.
relevant_for:
- Clinical psychologists and neuropsychologists counseling patients on cognitive health strategies
- Primary care physicians making lifestyle recommendations for cognitive preservation
- Researchers conducting or reviewing exercise-cognition randomized controlled trials
- Public health professionals developing physical activity guidelines
- Meta-analysts assessing publication bias and between-study heterogeneity

title: Detecting facial signals in conversational corpora using OpenFace compared to manual annotation: Challenges and recommendations
authors: [Naomi Nota, James Trujillo, Judith Holler]
summary:
This study evaluated the accuracy of OpenFace 2.2.0, an open-source facial behavior analysis toolkit, for detecting communicatively relevant facial signals in naturalistic face-to-face conversation. The authors compared OpenFace's automatic Action Unit (AU) annotations against 34,413 manual annotations produced by five trained annotators using a validated facial signal coding scheme applied to the CoAct corpus, a large naturalistic Dutch conversation dataset. AUs were mapped to nine facial signal categories (eyebrow frowns, eyebrow raises, blinks, smiles, squints, nose wrinkles, pressed lips, mouth corners down, and frown raises), and evaluated using Jaccard similarity, precision, recall, F1 score, and mean absolute difference (Mabs) for onset timing across 15 threshold conditions.

Human-human interrater reliability was generally good for frequent signals: average Krippendorff's alpha was 0.61 across all signals, with six signals reaching moderate or higher agreement (alpha 0.69-0.90) and average Jaccard similarity of 53%. For the subset of signals with moderate reliability, human-human Jaccard similarity averaged 74%. In stark contrast, the best-performing OpenFace model (dual-model approach with intensity threshold 0.9 and 100 ms timing threshold) achieved a mean F1 of only 0.126 across all signals, with mean Jaccard similarity of 12.6%. Performance varied considerably by signal: blinks were best detected (Jaccard = 0.26, F1 = 0.411), while pressed lips (F1 = 0.045) and mouth corners down (F1 = 0.085) were nearly undetectable. Even for well-detected signals, onset timing accuracy was poor (blink Mabs = 438 ms; smile Mabs = 1,291 ms). The dual-model approach (intersection of detection and intensity models) marginally outperformed the intensity-only model. The 100 ms minimum duration threshold generally outperformed the 40 ms threshold despite manual annotations using 40 ms, suggesting a lower bound on temporal resolution gains.

The authors attribute poor performance primarily to high false positive rates at lenient thresholds and substantial false negatives at stricter thresholds, with no single parameter setting providing strong detection across all signals. They provide seven concrete recommendations including calibration of intensity thresholds per signal, visual checking of subsets, using the dual-model approach, and noting that training AU models on spontaneous conversational data with varied viewing angles is likely required for reliable results. A validated facial signal annotation manual and Python code for custom thresholding are provided as supplementary materials.
clinical_insight: While this is primarily a methods paper in multimodal communication research, it has direct implications for clinical researchers who use automated facial expression analysis to study emotion, social interaction, or psychopathology. Many studies in clinical psychology employ tools like OpenFace to quantify facial affect in populations such as individuals with autism, depression, or social anxiety. The findings caution strongly against using OpenFace out-of-the-box for detecting communicatively meaningful facial signals in naturalistic settings, as even the best-performing configuration yielded F1 scores well below what trained annotators achieve. Clinical researchers relying on automated facial coding should implement quality checking procedures, calibrate thresholds on their own data, and consider manual annotation verification -- especially for subtle or infrequent signals relevant to clinical populations.
relevant_for:
- Researchers using automated facial expression analysis in clinical or social interaction studies
- Methodologists developing or validating computer vision tools for behavioral science
- Multimodal communication researchers studying pragmatic functions of facial signals
- Clinical psychologists studying nonverbal behavior in autism, mood disorders, or social anxiety
- Teams building large conversational corpora with automated behavioral annotation pipelines

title: Putting the brain back in its body: Integrating internal bodily signals in multimodal language research
authors: [Simone Gastaldon]
summary:
This perspective article argues that the embodied turn in language science should extend from the externally visible body (gesture, gaze, facial movement) to each interlocutor's internal bodily dynamics -- cardiac, respiratory, gastric, and arousal-linked signals. Gastaldon proposes that self-generated visceral signals serve three critical functions for a theory of conversation: gating the uptake of incoming communicative signals, regulating the timing of outgoing action including turn onset, and coupling across interacting individuals. The paper synthesizes evidence from the rapidly developing body-brain dynamics literature and applies it to face-to-face communication using Holler and Kuhlen's (2026) predictive framework as a reference.

Respiration is positioned as the central pivot channel because it is the most directly tied to speech production and is under partial voluntary control. Every utterance is produced on an out-breath; speakers inhale at grammatical and motor-planning junctures, and cognitive-linguistic demand reshapes speech breathing by reducing syllables per breath group. The cardiac cycle and pupil-linked arousal provide the gain mechanism: fearful faces are detected more accurately at systole, cardiac phase modulates multisensory integration and gaze reading, and voluntary actions are preferentially initiated at particular cardiac phases. Crucially, directed-connectivity analyses show breathing drives pupillary dynamics (respiration-pupil coupling at ~0.2-0.3 Hz) rather than the reverse, placing respiration upstream of the noradrenergic arousal system. The slower gastric rhythm (~0.05 Hz) phase-couples to cortical alpha amplitude, driving sensory and motor cortical regions that receive a partner's signals and orchestrate articulation.

The paper interprets these findings through predictive coding: bodily rhythms modulate local and global neural gain, shaping the precision assigned to predictions and prediction errors for both listeners and speakers. Four concrete components are proposed to add to current processing accounts: an endogenous gain term on both comprehension and production sides, a bodily production threshold, active rather than passive sampling (the body adjusts its own sampling rhythm), and inter-individual physiological coupling as a measurable variable. The framework generates falsifiable predictions, such as that turn-onset latency is partly a bodily variable influenced by cardiac and respiratory phase, and that bodily states weight the predictive value of multimodal signals including co-speech gestures and visible speech articulation.
clinical_insight: This perspective has substantial clinical relevance for understanding how somatic states shape communication. The framework directly connects to conditions where internal bodily dysregulation disrupts social interaction. In Parkinson's disease, respiratory constraints on speech are well-documented; in autism, atypical interoceptive processing may alter how internal rhythms gate the uptake of social signals. The proposal that inter-individual physiological coupling is a measurable variable in conversation opens new avenues for assessing social connectedness in conditions like depression (where diminished physiological synchrony has been observed) or anxiety (where heightened arousal may create imbalanced gain on threat-related social cues). The emphasis on respiration as a modifiable, partially voluntary channel under active control also connects to breathing-based interventions in clinical practice, suggesting a mechanistic account for why such approaches may facilitate communicative function beyond their effects on arousal reduction alone.
relevant_for:
- Researchers studying the neurobiology of face-to-face communication and turn-taking
- Clinical scientists investigating somatic contributions to social interaction deficits
- Psycholinguists interested in predictive processing and multimodal language comprehension
- Teams exploring physiological synchrony in dyadic interactions (e.g., depression, autism, PTSD)
- Researchers developing embodied or interoception-based models of psychopathology

title: Self-Coherence as a Higher-Order Organizational Process: Toward an Integrative Theory of Selfhood
authors: [Laura Barca]
summary:
This theoretical paper proposes self-coherence as a higher-order organizational principle of selfhood, distinct from self-regulation, and integrates perspectives from embodied cognition, predictive processing, phenomenology, identity research, and constructivist approaches to personality. Barca argues that the primary challenge of selfhood is not stability but continuity through change -- maintaining a coherent sense of self across bodily transformations, relational shifts, and developmental transitions. Self-coherence is defined as the dynamic capacity to maintain continuity and intelligibility of experience across time through the integration of embodied, affective, relational, and autobiographical processes, and is positioned not as an additional self-construct but as the organizational process through which multiple self-related mechanisms are coordinated.

The framework identifies four complementary mechanisms supporting self-coherence. Embodied processes provide a foundational sense of continuity through interoceptive signals (heartbeat, respiration, visceral sensations) that link bodily states to past memories, present awareness, and future expectations via predictive processing. Identity processes support integration across temporal horizons, connecting past experiences with present commitments and future aspirations through narrative and autobiographical reasoning. Personal Meaning Organizations (PMOs), drawn from the constructivist tradition (Arciero and Bondolfi, 2009; Guidano, 1987), are reinterpreted as strategies of temporal self-maintenance through which continuity is preserved despite change. Self-regulatory mechanisms are reconceptualized as serving the broader organizational goal of maintaining self-coherence rather than being ends in themselves. A key conceptual distinction is drawn between self-coherence and self-regulation: whereas regulation supports adaptive responding to current demands, coherence concerns the preservation of continuity and intelligibility across the full span of experience.

The paper draws on phenomenological psychiatry (Fuchs, 2013, 2017) to argue that psychopathology often involves disruptions in the temporal organization of experience rather than merely cognitive or emotional disturbance. Within a predictive processing framework, uncertainty becomes psychologically significant not merely because it impairs prediction but because it threatens self-coherence -- the capacity to maintain a coherent model of oneself across time. This reformulation helps explain why maladaptive behaviors (restrictive eating, compulsive rituals, reassurance seeking, hypervigilance toward bodily sensations) persist despite costs: they provide temporary experiences of predictability and continuity when ordinary self-coherence mechanisms become fragile. The paper generates testable hypotheses for future research, including whether self-coherence disruptions represent a transdiagnostic vulnerability and whether therapeutic change involves restoring continuity across embodied, relational, and autobiographical dimensions.
clinical_insight: This framework offers a reconceptualization of personality pathology and transdiagnostic processes that may resonate with clinicians working with personality disorders. The proposal that maladaptive behaviors function as attempts to restore self-coherence -- rather than merely regulate anxiety or reduce prediction error -- provides a parsimonious explanation for treatment-resistant patterns across multiple diagnostic categories. For borderline personality disorder, the framework connects identity diffusion and unstable relationships to failures of temporal integration; for obsessive-compulsive patterns, rituals are reinterpreted as coherence-maintenance strategies; for eating disorders, the author's prior work on active inference and interoceptive control (Barca and Pezzulo, 2020; Barca et al., 2023) is extended into this broader organizational account. The transdiagnostic framing suggests that therapeutic approaches should target not just symptom reduction but the restoration of continuity across embodied, relational, and autobiographical dimensions of experience, potentially informing integrative treatment planning.
relevant_for:
- Personality disorder researchers and clinicians seeking integrative theoretical frameworks
- Investigators of transdiagnostic processes in psychopathology
- Researchers studying selfhood, identity, and narrative coherence
- Clinical scientists interested in predictive processing and active inference models of mental disorder
- Those working at the intersection of embodiment, interoception, and personality

title: We Love Our Jobs! Systems Thinking and Participatory Action Research for the Future of Neurodiversity and Skilled Employment
authors: Dora M. Raymaker, Shawna Spain, Nybelle Caruso, Joelle Maslak, Nicole Leblanc, Morenike Giwa Onaiwu, Jonathan Spector, Passion Ilea, Julia Crown, Kristin Akervall
summary: This participatory action research study convened a panel of neurodivergent lived experience and employment experts to address the complex problem of neurodivergent under- and unemployment in skilled professions. Using qualitative systems thinking methods including Soft Systems Methodology, futures visioning with SWOT analysis, and Group Model Building (system dynamics), the workshops identified key leverage points for improving neurodivergent employment outcomes. Rather than focusing on remediating individual disability characteristics or building specific skills, the study found that intervention leverage lies at the intersection of organizational psychology constructs, specifically employee wellbeing, workplace climate, and mutually beneficial workplace relationships, combined with a new disability stress construct. The authors recommend examining the organizational psychology literature for adaptable interventions, focusing on peer support, leveraging emerging technology to manage complexity, and supporting supervisor behavior change grounded in authentic understanding of neurodivergent experience. The study highlights that reaching a future where neurodivergent people thrive professionally requires systemic changes across education, resources, infrastructure, universal access, paradigm shifts, and recognition of individual and social complexity.
clinical_insight: This study shifts the clinical framing of neurodivergent employment support from individual deficit remediation to systemic organizational intervention. For clinicians working with neurodivergent adults, the findings suggest that vocational support should focus less on skills training and social masking, and more on assessing workplace climate factors, facilitating peer support networks, and addressing disability stress. The identification of a disability stress construct provides a new lens for understanding the cumulative toll of navigating neurotypical workplaces, which can inform both assessment and formulation. Clinicians can use the systems thinking framework to help clients identify leverage points in their own employment situations rather than solely working on individual coping strategies.
relevant_for: Clinicians supporting neurodivergent adults in workplace navigation, vocational rehabilitation counselors, organizational psychologists, and therapists working with autistic and ADHD adults experiencing occupational difficulties or burnout.

title: How to Implement Interdisciplinary Care in Persistent Pain: A Process-Based Framework for Clinical Decision-Making
authors: Klara Albajes, Jenny Moix, Lester Jones
summary: This paper operationalizes the Process-Based Interdisciplinary Therapy (PbIT) framework for clinical decision-making in persistent pain management. PbIT integrates three theoretical models into a recursive clinical decision-making system: the Pain and Movement Reasoning Model (PMRM) for identifying modifiable biopsychosocial processes, micro-level functional analysis for understanding process-behavior relationships, and the Extended Evolutionary Meta-Model (EEMM) for guiding intervention selection. The framework is implemented through a six-step clinical workflow encompassing interdisciplinary assessment, network formulation, functional analysis, target prioritization, intervention design, continuous monitoring, and readjustment. A hypothetical clinical case demonstrates how PbIT supports collaborative reasoning and adaptive treatment planning across disciplines. Rather than prescribing discipline-specific interventions, the framework provides a flexible structure for coordinating professional expertise around modifiable processes identified through interdisciplinary assessment, making interdisciplinary clinical reasoning explicit, transparent, and reproducible.
clinical_insight: This framework addresses a persistent gap in persistent pain care: the lack of operational guidance for how multidisciplinary teams should integrate their assessments and coordinate treatment decisions. For clinicians working in pain management, PbIT offers a concrete decision-making workflow that moves beyond the biopsychosocial model as a conceptual framework into an actionable clinical tool. The integration of network formulation is particularly valuable, as it allows teams to visualize how psychological processes such as fear-avoidance, catastrophizing, and attentional focus interact with movement behaviors and social factors. The recursive monitoring and readjustment step ensures that treatment remains adaptive rather than following rigid protocols. Clinicians can apply the six-step workflow even in settings with limited interdisciplinary resources by using it as a structured reasoning tool for case conceptualization and treatment planning.
relevant_for: Pain management clinicians, clinical psychologists working with chronic pain, physiotherapists, occupational therapists, and multidisciplinary pain clinic teams seeking structured frameworks for coordinated care.

title: A Cultural-Ecosocial Framework for Brain Health Research
authors: Laurence J. Kirmayer, David Benrimoh, Boris C. Bernhardt, Laurette Dube, Guillaume Dumas, Maiya R. Geddes, Tasmia Hai, Robert Paul Juster, Jai Shah, Ashley Wazana
summary: Kirmayer and colleagues propose a comprehensive cultural-ecosocial framework for brain health research that systematically integrates social and cultural context into neuroscience. The 40-page paper argues that despite enormous investment in neuroscience, most research uses nonrepresentative samples and ignores contextual factors that shape brain health, limiting the translational value of findings. The framework is organized around six key perspectives: (1) a cultural-ecosocial systems view situating the brain within layered systems from body to society, (2) social determinants of brain health as interacting processes and structures rather than decontextualized lists, (3) a lifespan developmental perspective capturing changing dynamics from prenatal to old age, (4) diversity and intersectionality acknowledging how identity, social position, and cultural differences shape health trajectories, (5) precision-convergence science using complex systems modelling and AI alongside neuroinformatics, and (6) a person-centered approach integrating lived experience and self-understanding. The framework draws on Bronfenbrenner's ecological systems theory, 4E cognitive science (embodied, embedded, enacted, extended), social epidemiology, and cultural phenomenology. The authors provide concrete operational recommendations for brain health studies: situate the brain within nested systems, model structural social determinants alongside biological variables, consider developmental timing, ensure sample representativeness, incorporate multilevel analytic strategies, and include person-centered outcome measures such as the WHO-5 alongside neurobiological measures. The paper critiques three types of brain health definitions (physical health focus, cognitive performance focus, and well-being focus) and advocates for the WHO's comprehensive definition encompassing cognitive, sensory, social-emotional, behavioral, and motor domains.
clinical_insight: For clinical practitioners, this framework provides a rigorous justification for incorporating social and cultural assessment into clinical work with neurological and psychiatric populations. The emphasis on social determinants as causal mechanisms rather than mere correlates reinforces the importance of assessing housing stability, discrimination exposure, educational access, and social connectedness as part of clinical formulation. The person-centered perspective, which treats individuals' self-understanding and meaning-making as active contributors to health outcomes rather than epiphenomena, supports narrative and formulation-based therapeutic approaches. The framework also challenges clinicians to consider how cultural models of illness that patients bring to treatment can create looping effects that shape disease processes through feedback between self-interpretation and biology. Additionally, the recommendation to measure positive outcomes like well-being alongside symptom reduction aligns with recovery-oriented and strengths-based clinical practice, and supports the use of validated instruments like the WHO-5 to track broader recovery dimensions beyond symptom remission.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists, psychiatrists, and neuropsychologists working with diverse populations, clinicians interested in cultural competence and social determinants of health, researchers designing clinical studies that aim for real-world generalizability, and trainees in cultural psychiatry.

title: Diving into the Stigma Around Consensual Non-Monogamies: Comparing Psychopathology, Well-Being, Attachment, and Other Psychological Characteristics with Monogamous Counterparts
authors: Oscar Lecuona, Magaly Garcia-Senlle, Paula Serrano, Rocio Iturri, Irene Martinez-Cuevas, Mireya Brito, Sara de Rivas
summary: This cross-sectional study compared 815 Spanish adults (388 consensual non-monogamy practitioners, 427 monogamous) across psychopathology, well-being, attachment, dark personality traits, spirituality, and empathy using validated self-report measures. ANCOVAs controlling for age and LGBTIQA+ status revealed no robust overall differences in psychopathology or dark personality traits, directly refuting maladjustment stereotypes. CNM practitioners reported significantly higher sexual sensation seeking, resilience, autonomy, and personal growth alongside lower personal distress, though all effect sizes were small. A critical finding was the significant interaction between relationship style and LGBTQIA+ identity: monogamous LGBTQIA+ individuals exhibited the highest levels of anxious attachment and psychopathology (particularly depression, hostility-aggression, and paranoia), while CNM LGBTQIA+ individuals showed lower levels, suggesting CNM communities may buffer minority stress through more affirming social contexts. CNM individuals scored higher on social desirability, consistent with compensatory impression management under stigma. The study was published in the International Journal of Sexual Health.
clinical_insight: For couples therapists and sexologists, these findings provide empirical backing to challenge pathologizing assumptions about CNM clients. Clinicians should be aware that presenting problems in CNM clients are more likely related to minority stress, stigma management, and identity concealment than to relational structure per se. The finding that LGBTQIA+ monogamous individuals showed the highest psychopathology and attachment anxiety suggests that clinicians working with sexual minority clients should not assume monogamy is protective. The compensatory social desirability bias in CNM respondents highlights the importance of creating non-judgmental therapeutic spaces where clients feel safe disclosing their relationship structure. Assessment and formulation should consider the intersecting effects of relational orientation and sexual/gender minority status rather than treating CNM as a risk factor.
relevant_for: Couples therapists, sexologists, and clinicians working with LGBTQIA+ clients. Relevant for clinical training on non-monogamous relationship structures and reducing therapist bias in formulation.

title: Predicting Early Non-Response to Buprenorphine Treatment for Opioid Use Disorder: A Machine Learning Approach
authors: Victoria Votaw, Mary McNamara, Emily Levine, Allen J. Bailey, Maureen Hillhouse, R. Kathryn McHugh
summary: This study developed and tested machine learning models to predict early non-response to buprenorphine treatment for opioid use disorder using data from two harmonized clinical trials (N=562). Early non-response was defined as either 2+ days of illicit opioid use in the first three weeks or a craving score of 2+ on a 0-10 VAS in the first week. Elastic net and random forest models were trained using pre-treatment demographics and clinical variables including polysubstance use, treatment history, and baseline severity. The models aimed to identify patients at risk of poor early response, enabling earlier clinical intervention or treatment adjustment.
clinical_insight: Early non-response to buprenorphine is a robust predictor of longer-term treatment failure, making this prediction task clinically meaningful. For clinicians managing OUD treatment, the ability to identify at-risk patients within the first weeks of treatment could enable proactive dose adjustments, adjunctive psychosocial interventions, or earlier transition to alternative medications like methadone or extended-release naltrexone. The machine learning approach allows integration of multiple risk factors simultaneously rather than relying on single clinical indicators. If validated, such prediction tools could be integrated into clinical decision support systems at addiction treatment clinics.
relevant_for: Addiction psychiatrists, clinical psychologists in substance use treatment, and treatment program administrators interested in precision medicine approaches to OUD.

title: Tendencies that Help You Bend but Not Break: A Prospective Co-Twin Control Study of Personality Factors Predicting Resilience Outcomes
authors: Live Skow Hofgaard, Ragnhild Bang Nes, Ziada Ayorech, Olav Mandt Vassend, Ludvig Daae Bjorndal, Espen Roysamb
summary: This prospective co-twin control study examined associations between Big Five personality traits and their facets with two types of outcome-based resilience over a 6-year interval, using a Norwegian Twin Registry subsample born 1945-1960 (N=1,516 in 2010; N=1,987 in 2016). Personality was measured via NEO-PI-R in 2010, and two resilience outcomes were operationalized as standardized regression residuals after regressing mental health indicators on lifetime cumulative adversity: type 1 (less anxiety/depressive symptoms than predicted) and type 2 (higher life satisfaction than predicted). In the full sample, neuroticism and most of its facets showed significant prospective associations with both resilience types. After co-twin control for genetic and shared environmental confounding, neuroticism facets (particularly anxiety, depression, and stress reactivity) remained significant for both types, the extraversion facet positive emotions predicted type 1 resilience, and conscientiousness with its facet achievement striving predicted type 2 resilience. The heritability of resilience was estimated at 24-52%, and personality-resilience associations showed substantial genetic overlap, with the co-twin control reducing but not eliminating associations for key facets.
clinical_insight: For clinicians, this study provides robust evidence that personality characteristics associated with neuroticism are the strongest and most reliable prospective predictors of resilience, even after controlling for genetic confounding. This supports targeting anxiety, depression proneness, and stress reactivity in therapy to enhance resilience, rather than focusing solely on positive traits. The distinction between type 1 resilience (avoiding illbeing) and type 2 resilience (promoting wellbeing) is clinically useful: neuroticism-reducing interventions may primarily support the former, while conscientiousness-building approaches may support the latter. The finding that many personality-resilience associations are genetically confounded cautions against interpreting cross-sectional personality assessments as direct targets for intervention without considering the complex etiology. However, the associations that survived co-twin control suggest that environmentally mediated personality change, such as through psychotherapy, could genuinely influence resilience outcomes.
relevant_for: Clinical psychologists interested in resilience, personality assessment in therapy, and treatment planning for stress-related disorders. Relevant for understanding which personality factors are causally relevant versus genetically confounded in treatment outcomes.

title: Acoustic Startle Reactivity and Pre-Pulse Inhibition in ADHD and Autism: Evidence for a Developmental Emergence of Group Differences
authors: Ala Seif, Renee Guerville, Mohammed Rajab, Kristina Schaaf, Samantha Schulz, Susanne Schmid, Ryan Stevenson
summary: This transdiagnostic study examined acoustic startle response amplitude and pre-pulse inhibition (PPI) at 60ms and 120ms stimulus onset asynchronies in 154 children and adults across three diagnostic groups: ADHD (n=47), ASD (n=43), and typically developing controls (n=64), using linear mixed-effects models with diagnostic group, age group, sex, and interactions as predictors. In children, no significant group differences emerged in either startle amplitude or PPI. In adults, the ASD group showed reduced PPI at the 120ms SOA compared to controls, while the ADHD group did not differ significantly from controls. These findings suggest that sensorimotor gating differences in ASD emerge developmentally rather than being present from childhood, and that ADHD and ASD show distinct neurophysiological profiles on startle measures. The study provides evidence against simple hyper-reactivity models of sensory processing differences in neurodevelopmental conditions.
clinical_insight: These findings have direct implications for how clinicians understand and assess sensory processing differences in neurodivergent clients. The absence of group differences in children suggests that acoustic startle reactivity and PPI may not be useful as early diagnostic biomarkers for ASD or ADHD in childhood, despite theoretical expectations. The emergence of PPI differences in adults with ASD but not ADHD supports the idea that these conditions have distinct neurophysiological trajectories. For occupational therapists and clinicians working with sensory sensitivities in neurodivergent adults, the reduced PPI in adult ASD suggests specific sensorimotor gating difficulties that may inform sensory-based interventions. The developmental emergence pattern also cautions against generalizing findings from adult studies to child populations in clinical assessment.
relevant_for: Clinicians assessing neurodivergent adults, occupational therapists working with sensory processing, and researchers interested in neurophysiological markers in ASD and ADHD.

title: Reactivity, Habituation, and Initial Value Dependence of the Acoustic Startle Response in a Transdiagnostic Developmental Sample
authors: Ala Seif, Renee Guerville, Mohammed Rajab, Kristina Schaaf, Samantha Schulz, Susanne Schmid, Ryan Stevenson
summary: This study examined eyeblink EMG startle habituation and Initial Value Dependence (IVD), the tendency for larger initial responses to predict steeper habituation slopes, in 165 individuals (93 children, 72 adults) across typically developing (n=63), ASD (n=54), and ADHD (n=48) diagnostic groups. IVD has been proposed as a marker of physiological flexibility but has rarely been examined in developmental or clinical populations. The study provides normative data on startle habituation across development and diagnostic groups, establishing whether IVD operates as a consistent individual-difference marker. This companion paper to m24sc provides the habituation and IVD analyses while the other focuses on PPI.
clinical_insight: Initial Value Dependence as a measure of physiological flexibility could offer clinicians an objective biomarker for assessing stress responsivity and adaptability across clinical populations. If IVD proves reliable, it could supplement self-report measures of stress reactivity in assessment batteries, particularly for clients where introspective accuracy is limited. The transdiagnostic developmental approach provides a model for how psychophysiological measures can be used to understand shared and distinct mechanisms across neurodevelopmental conditions, informing personalized assessment strategies.
relevant_for: Clinical neuropsychologists, researchers using psychophysiological measures in assessment, and clinicians interested in objective markers of stress responsivity.

title: Behavioral Biomarker Reliability Can Mislead Mechanistic Inference in Precision Psychiatry
authors: Kenny Yu, Maria M. Robinson, Wolf Vanpaemel, Francis Tuerlinckx, Jonas Zaman
summary: Through systematic simulations of a fear-conditioning task using a Rescorla-Wagner generative model, this study demonstrates two ways behavioral biomarker reliability can mislead mechanistic inference in precision psychiatry. First, common descriptive summaries (e.g., learning slopes, differential scores) can appear unreliable because they are shaped by experimental context such as trial order rather than the target mechanism, or can appear reliable because they capture stable but unintended features of responding like baseline responsivity rather than the intended learning process. Second, computational model parameters can achieve substantially higher reliability than descriptive summaries, but only when the model formalization matches the data-generating process and the task design provides enough informative prediction errors for parameter recovery. The paper identifies five specific problems: summaries failing to separate learners within sessions (Problem 1), appearing unreliable despite stable learning due to trial order effects (Problem 2), appearing reliable while tracking response traits rather than learning (Problem 3), model parameters failing when the model assumes the wrong mechanism (Problem 4), and model parameters failing when auxiliary processes like inter-session memory carry-over are omitted (Problem 5). The authors demonstrate these signatures in real datasets from fear conditioning and the Iowa Gambling Task, and argue that behavioral biomarker validation should follow a different order: first specify the target mechanism, then verify the task makes it observable, then check whether the analysis can recover it, with reliability as the final check rather than the first.
clinical_insight: For clinicians and researchers developing or using behavioral tasks for treatment selection, patient stratification, or outcome monitoring, this paper provides a critical framework for evaluating whether task-derived measures actually measure what they claim. The finding that standard fear-conditioning summary scores can be reliable without tracking the intended learning mechanism means that a biomarker passing reliability thresholds may still be clinically meaningless. Conversely, low reliability of a summary score does not necessarily mean the underlying process is unmeasurable. This has direct implications for precision psychiatry efforts to use behavioral tasks like fear conditioning for treatment selection in anxiety disorders. Clinicians should be skeptical of task-based biomarker claims that rely solely on reliability and prediction evidence without demonstrating mechanism recovery. The paper advocates for parameter-recovery simulation as a routine quality check, analogous to power analysis, before deploying behavioral measures in clinical contexts.
relevant_for: Researchers developing behavioral biomarkers for precision psychiatry, clinical psychologists using fear conditioning or other cognitive tasks for assessment or treatment selection, and methodologists interested in computational psychiatry.

title: Shaping New Perceptions: A Preliminary Multi-Method Investigation of Changes in Hostile Attributions following a Psychoeducational Mentalization-Based Treatment Module
authors: Marta Bodecka-Zych, Dorota Jasielska, Monika Jańczak, Joanna Rajchert, Jean Gagnon, Anna Zajenkowska
summary:
This pilot study investigated whether a brief psychoeducational mentalization-based treatment (MBT) module could reduce hostile attribution bias across four distinct populations: incarcerated adult males, juvenile delinquents, high school students, and young adults from the general community. A total of 58 participants across the four groups completed a six-session MBT psychoeducational program delivered across three meetings, with pre- and post-assessment using multiple measurement modalities including ambiguous facial expression recognition, visual scene interpretation, the Ambiguous Intentions and Hostility Questionnaire (AIHQ), and self-recalled interpersonal situations.

Results indicated that the MBT module was associated with reduced hostile attributions among young adults and juvenile offenders, suggesting the intervention holds promise for populations prone to interpreting ambiguous social cues as threatening. The effects were most consistent for young adults from the general population, while juvenile delinquents also showed meaningful reductions in hostile attribution tendencies. High school students demonstrated mixed results, with some measures showing improvement and others not reaching significance, potentially reflecting developmental variability in mentalizing capacity. Incarcerated adults showed changes only for specific stimulus formats, suggesting that entrenched hostile attribution patterns in this population may require more intensive or longer-duration interventions.

A qualitative analysis of participant responses revealed themes of increased perspective-taking and reduced automatic hostile interpretations following the MBT module. Participants described greater awareness of alternative explanations for others' behaviour and a reduced tendency to default to hostile attributions in ambiguous social situations. The multi-method assessment approach provided converging evidence that brief mentalization-focused psychoeducation can shift social-cognitive processing, though the effects varied by population and measurement method.

The study was funded by the National Science Centre Poland through three separate grants (2017/26/D/HS6/00258, 2021/42/E/HS6/00018, and 2021/41/N/HS6/01111). The authors acknowledge the preliminary nature of the findings and the small sample sizes within each group, calling for larger randomized controlled trials to confirm and extend these results.
clinical_insight:
This study demonstrates that even a very brief, psychoeducational MBT module can produce measurable shifts in hostile attribution bias, a key cognitive risk factor for aggression and interpersonal violence. The multi-method approach strengthens confidence in the findings by showing convergence across self-report, behavioural, and qualitative measures. Clinicians working in forensic and correctional settings may find particular value in these results, as hostile attribution bias is a well-established target for reducing recidivism and aggressive behaviour.

The differential effects across populations are clinically informative: the fact that incarcerated adults showed the most limited response suggests that brief psychoeducation alone may be insufficient for individuals with deeply entrenched patterns of hostile social cognition, and that more intensive or trauma-informed mentalization work may be needed. The positive findings in juvenile offenders are especially promising given the developmental window for intervention in adolescence.
relevant_for:
- Forensic and correctional psychologists working with hostile attribution bias
- Clinicians delivering brief psychoeducational interventions in institutional settings
- Researchers studying mentalization-based approaches to aggression reduction
- Practitioners working with juvenile justice populations
- Those interested in multi-method assessment of social-cognitive change

title: Undue Influence as Dynamic Constraint of Agency-Space: A Mechanism-Level Theory of Conscious Agency Under Control
authors: T. Dawson Woodrum
summary:
This theoretical article proposes Agency-Space Theory, a novel mechanism-level account of undue influence that reconceptualizes how conscious agency becomes constrained under conditions of coercive control. The theory distinguishes between overt behaviour and the internal "agency-space" within which voluntary action is experienced, arguing that voluntary-appearing compliance can coexist with profoundly constrained agency — a critical distinction for understanding how victims of coercive control can appear to act freely while their decision-making capacity has been systematically narrowed.

The theory defines seven linked operations through which undue influence constrains agency-space: boundary-condition manipulation (controlling the information and social environment), epistemic narrowing (restricting what is knowable or considered), salience conditioning (shaping what attention is drawn to), meaning-model reorganization (altering how events are interpreted), relational survivability compression (making the controlling relationship feel existentially necessary), reflective-access restriction (limiting introspective awareness of the constraint), and stabilization of externally preferred outcomes as internally experienced necessity (making the controller's preferences feel like one's own choices). These operations are described as dynamically linked, with each reinforcing the others in a self-sustaining system.

A key prediction of the theory is hysteresis — the phenomenon whereby constraint of agency-space persists even after an individual has exited the controlling environment. This helps explain the well-documented clinical observation that survivors of coercive control, cult involvement, or abusive relationships often continue to show restricted agency and difficulty making autonomous decisions long after the external control has ended. The theory treats cognitive dissonance, demoralization, and dissociation not as separate phenomena but as local processes that naturally emerge within the broader dynamics of agency-space constraint.

The framework is applied to multiple contexts including coercive control in intimate relationships, cult dynamics, abusive organisational environments, and critically, therapeutic contexts where power dynamics operate. The author discusses how the theory bridges gaps between existing accounts of undue influence, coercion, and related clinical phenomena, offering a unified mechanistic language for describing how agency becomes constrained across diverse settings.
clinical_insight:
Agency-Space Theory offers clinicians a sophisticated framework for understanding why survivors of coercive control often present with symptoms that do not fit neatly into existing diagnostic categories. The concept of hysteresis is particularly clinically relevant, as it provides a theoretical basis for the persistent difficulties in autonomous decision-making, self-trust, and agency that characterise many survivors long after leaving abusive situations. Therapists working with trauma, particularly complex trauma involving interpersonal control, may find this framework useful for conceptualising treatment targets and explaining patients' experiences to them.

The theory's application to therapeutic contexts is especially important: it invites clinicians to reflect on how their own positional power and the inherent authority dynamics of therapy could inadvertently constrain a patient's agency-space, even in well-intentioned treatment. This self-reflective dimension makes the theory valuable for supervision and ethical practice.
relevant_for:
- Trauma therapists working with survivors of coercive control and domestic abuse
- Clinicians treating individuals exiting cults or high-demand groups
- Psychologists interested in agency, autonomy, and volition
- Forensic assessors evaluating undue influence cases
- Supervisors and trainers in ethical therapeutic practice

title: Do depression stigma and literacy differ between military personnel and the general population? Evidence from a matched comparison of the German Armed Forces (Bundeswehr) and the general population
authors: Caroline Allenhof, Hanna Reich, Andreas Czaplicki, Anna-Lena Nadine Jasmin Leimberger, Jil Zippelius, Annika Heer, Ulrich Hegerl, Gerd Dieter Willmund
summary:
This cross-sectional study compared depression stigma and depression literacy between German Armed Forces (Bundeswehr) military personnel and the general German population using a propensity-score matched design. The military sample comprised 1,964 individuals from two subgroups — operational military personnel and military university affiliates — while the matched general population sample included 15,535 participants. The study used the Depression Stigma Scale (measuring both personal and perceived stigma on 0–36 scales) and the Multifaceted Depression Literacy Scale (M-DL, 0–12) as primary outcome measures, with the trial registered under DRKS00026948.

The results revealed a nuanced pattern: operational military personnel reported significantly lower personal and perceived depression stigma compared to the general population (p ≤ .030), a somewhat counterintuitive finding that may reflect military mental health awareness campaigns or cultural factors specific to the Bundeswehr. However, military university affiliates did not differ from the general population on either stigma dimension. Neither military subgroup differed from the general population in depression literacy scores, suggesting that while stigma may be reduced in some military contexts, knowledge about depression has not correspondingly improved.

Within-group analyses identified important demographic predictors. Mid-aged military personnel showed significantly lower depression literacy than their general population counterparts (p < .05). Across both military and general population samples, lower educational attainment and lack of personal contact with depression were associated with higher stigma and lower literacy. Within the military specifically, lower-ranked personnel and those who had never been deployed reported higher personal stigma (p ≤ .006) and lower depression literacy (p = .047), suggesting that the most vulnerable subgroups within the military may face the greatest barriers to mental health help-seeking.

The authors conclude that anti-stigma interventions within military settings should be specifically targeted toward mid-aged, lower-ranked, and never-deployed personnel, as these groups carry the highest stigma and lowest literacy. The finding that operational military personnel show lower stigma than the general population is discussed in the context of recent Bundeswehr mental health promotion efforts, though the lack of corresponding literacy improvements highlights a gap between attitude change and knowledge acquisition.
clinical_insight:
This study has direct implications for military mental health services and for clinicians working with armed forces populations. The identification of lower-ranked, mid-aged, and never-deployed personnel as high-risk groups for both elevated stigma and reduced depression literacy provides a clear evidence base for targeted psychoeducation and anti-stigma interventions. The finding that anti-stigma efforts may reduce negative attitudes without improving actual knowledge about depression is an important caution for programme designers — attitude change alone is insufficient if service members lack the literacy to recognise depression in themselves or others.

For civilian clinicians, the study illustrates how organisational culture and mental health campaigns can differentially affect stigma versus literacy, a distinction that is relevant across many institutional settings beyond the military.
relevant_for:
- Military mental health professionals and programme designers
- Clinicians developing anti-stigma and psychoeducational interventions
- Researchers studying depression literacy and help-seeking behaviour
- Public health professionals working with institutional populations
- Those interested in propensity-score matching methodology in mental health research

title: The risks of conflating anhedonia with apathy
authors: Sijia Zhao, Mercè Correa, John D. Salamone, Masud Husain
summary:
This perspective article argues that anhedonia — the inability to experience pleasure — and apathy — a significant reduction in goal-directed behaviour — are mechanistically distinct constructs that are increasingly and problematically conflated in both clinical practice and translational neuroscience research. The authors draw on neurobiological, computational, and clinical evidence to demonstrate that treating these phenomena as interchangeable has serious consequences for both assessment accuracy and therapeutic development.

The authors propose deconstructing reward-pursuit into three psychologically and neurobiologically distinct stages: liking (the hedonic impact of rewarding stimuli, primarily mediated by opioid systems), wanting (the incentive salience attributed to reward-related cues, primarily mediated by dopaminergic systems), and acting (the behavioural activation required to pursue rewards, involving dorsal striatal circuits and premotor networks). They demonstrate that much of what is labelled "anhedonia" in translational animal models and some clinical instruments actually reflects impaired behavioural activation — that is, apathy rather than a true reduction in pleasure capacity. This misclassification has led to a systematic bias in which motivational deficits are conceptualised as hedonic deficits, with corresponding implications for treatment development.

Clinical evidence is marshalled from several sources. Factor analysis of over 1,000 participants identifies five independent factors — anhedonia plus three apathy domains (behavioural, social, and emotional) — that are separable from general depression severity. The authors characterise "pure" apathy as marked by low self-initiation and reduced social engagement, while "pure" anhedonia is characterised specifically by blunted enjoyment. A striking illustration of the measurement problem comes from caregiver-patient discrepancy data in behavioural variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD): only 9% of patients self-report severe apathy compared to 58% as reported by caregivers, highlighting the limitations of self-report when the construct being measured (self-initiation) is itself impaired. Machine learning analyses reveal that as few as 10 symptoms can differentially classify apathy, anhedonia, and depression, underscoring that these are empirically separable.

The therapeutic implications are significant: the authors note that SSRIs, widely prescribed for depression, often fail to alleviate motivational deficits, which is consistent with the argument that apathy and anhedonia require different pharmacological targets. They argue that the conflation of these constructs has misdirected therapeutic development, as treatments designed to enhance pleasure processing will not help patients whose primary deficit lies in behavioural activation systems. The article was funded by the Wellcome Trust (226645/Z/22/Z), the NIHR, and the NIH/NIMH.
clinical_insight:
This article has profound implications for clinical assessment and treatment planning. Many clinicians routinely assess "anhedonia" using instruments that may actually be tapping apathy or general motivational deficits, leading to inaccurate case conceptualisation. The caregiver-patient discrepancy data in bvFTD is a powerful reminder that self-report measures of motivation and initiative are inherently problematic when the very capacity being measured is impaired. Clinicians should consider incorporating informant reports and behavioural observation alongside self-report when assessing reward-related deficits.

The therapeutic message is clear: if a patient presents with amotivation and reduced goal-directed behaviour, assuming this reflects anhedonia and prescribing an SSRI or a treatment focused on enhancing pleasure may be ineffective. A more precise differential assessment distinguishing liking, wanting, and acting deficits could guide more targeted interventions — for example, behavioural activation strategies, dopaminergic agents, or structured goal-setting approaches for patients whose primary deficit is in the "acting" stage rather than the "liking" stage.
relevant_for:
- Clinical neuropsychologists assessing reward-processing deficits
- Psychiatrists prescribing for depression and neurodegenerative conditions
- Researchers developing or validating measures of anhedonia and apathy
- Neuroscientists studying reward circuitry and translational models
- Clinicians working with bvFTD, Parkinson's disease, or schizophrenia populations

title: The Effects of a Yoga Intervention on Adults with Intellectual Disabilities (ID). A systematic review.
authors: Louise Gates
summary:
This systematic review examined the effects of yoga interventions on adults with intellectual disabilities (ID), searching across eight major databases including Cochrane, SCOPUS, Medline, AMED, CINHAL, SportsDiscus, PsychARTICLES, and PsychINFO. From an initial pool of 145 results, only a single study met the inclusion criteria — a striking finding that underscores the near-total absence of rigorous research in this area. The sole included study by Hawkins et al. (2012) employed a multiple baseline design with two young adults with mild intellectual disabilities who completed nine vinyasa yoga sessions.

Outcome measures in the included study included partial interval recording of exercise behaviour, the Eston-Parfitt Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) scale, and the Physical Activity Enjoyment Scale (PACES). Participant A's exercise behaviour increased substantially from a baseline of 50% to 97–100% following the yoga intervention, representing an effect size of 100%. Participant B showed a similar pattern, with exercise behaviour increasing to 98–100% post-intervention. Both participants demonstrated increased perceived exertion over the course of the yoga sessions and reported moderate levels of enjoyment that tended to increase across sessions, suggesting that yoga was both physically engaging and subjectively pleasant for these individuals.

The risk of bias assessment rated the single included study as medium risk, primarily due to selective reporting concerns. The review's conclusions are appropriately cautious: while the available evidence suggests that yoga shows preliminary promise for adults with intellectual disabilities — particularly in terms of increasing exercise behaviour and enjoyment — the evidence base is far too limited to draw firm conclusions. The author explicitly calls for rigorous randomised controlled trials with adequate sample sizes to properly evaluate the effects of yoga in this population.

The review contextualises the need for such research by noting that adults with intellectual disabilities experience higher rates of anxiety, depression, and obesity compared to the general population, highlighting the importance of developing accessible, non-pharmacological interventions. Yoga, as a adaptable and low-cost intervention, represents a promising avenue, but the current evidence gap means that clinical recommendations remain premature.
clinical_insight:
The near-absence of research on yoga for adults with intellectual disabilities is itself a clinically significant finding. Clinicians and service providers working with this population are left without an evidence base for a potentially valuable, low-cost, non-pharmacological intervention. The single study's positive findings regarding exercise behaviour and enjoyment are encouraging but far from sufficient to guide practice.

For clinicians working in ID services, this review highlights a broader pattern of exclusion of adults with intellectual disabilities from mainstream intervention research. The high rates of anxiety, depression, and obesity in this population create an urgent need for accessible non-pharmacological approaches. Clinicians may consider yoga as a potentially beneficial adjunctive activity while acknowledging the lack of evidence, and should advocate for inclusion of adults with ID in yoga and mind-body intervention research.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians and service providers working with adults with intellectual disabilities
- Researchers interested in mind-body interventions for underserved populations
- Yoga therapists and instructors working with special populations
- Occupational therapists seeking evidence-based activity programmes for ID populations
- Systematic reviewers studying intervention research gaps

title: Phenomenological-control framing of suggestion-based interventions: a testable hypothesis for acceptability and implementation in psychiatric care
authors: Adrian Sanger, Tadeusz Pietras
summary:
This hypothesis and theory article addresses a striking implementation gap: while suggestion-based interventions (commonly known as hypnosis) demonstrate robust clinical efficacy across multiple domains — an umbrella review encompassing 49 meta-analyses and 261 primary studies with more than 50% showing medium-to-large effect sizes in pain, irritable bowel syndrome, surgical preparation, and psychiatric conditions including anxiety, depression, and PTSD — only 8.3% of German anaesthesia departments actually use them in practice. The authors propose that the label "hypnosis" itself may be a primary barrier to implementation, and offer an alternative framing strategy.

The authors propose "phenomenological-control framing" — presenting suggestion-based interventions as leveraging the patient's own capacity for goal-directed regulation of subjective experience. This framing explicitly contrasts with the term "hypnosis," which the authors argue evokes persistent misconceptions among both patients and clinicians about mind control, passivity, loss of autonomy, and manipulation. Survey data reviewed in the article indicate that more than 50% of patients endorse misconceptions about hypnosis, and that health professionals incorrectly associate it with losing control and creating dependency. Critically, the hypnotic label may actively reduce treatment responding in some individuals through anticipatory resistance.

The article generates a set of testable predictions at both the patient level and the provider level. At the patient level, phenomenological-control framing is predicted to increase perceived agency, reduce loss-of-control concerns, improve acceptability ratings, and enhance willingness to engage with the intervention. At the provider level, the alternative framing is predicted to enhance perceived scientific credibility, perceived appropriateness, and intentions to recommend, refer, or adopt suggestion-based interventions in clinical practice. The authors emphasise that these predictions concern implementation and acceptability rather than efficacy — the argument is not that reframing makes the intervention more effective per se, but that it may remove barriers to patients receiving an already-evidence-based treatment.

The article situates these predictions within the broader context of implementation science and discusses the particular importance of agency-preserving language in psychiatric care, where therapeutic alliance, patient autonomy, and collaborative decision-making are especially valued. The authors argue that in contexts where patients may already feel vulnerable or where power dynamics are salient — including pain management, trauma treatment, and psychiatric care more broadly — presenting interventions in a way that emphasises the patient's own regulatory capacities may be both ethically preferable and practically more effective for uptake.
clinical_insight:
This article offers a practical, evidence-informed strategy for clinicians who use suggestion-based techniques but encounter patient or institutional resistance to the term "hypnosis." The proposed reframing is not merely semantic — it is grounded in documented misconceptions and predicted to have measurable effects on treatment acceptability and uptake. For psychiatrists, psychologists, and pain specialists who have found clinical hypnosis valuable but struggle with patient acceptance or institutional buy-in, phenomenological-control framing provides a theoretically justified alternative that may bridge the implementation gap.

The distinction between efficacy and acceptability is clinically crucial: an intervention can be highly effective but poorly utilised if patients refuse it or clinicians fail to offer it. By focusing specifically on the implementation barrier, this article provides actionable guidance that does not require changes to clinical technique, only to how the intervention is presented and discussed with patients.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians using hypnosis or suggestion-based interventions in practice
- Implementation scientists studying evidence-to-practice gaps
- Psychiatrists and psychologists working in pain management or trauma
- Medical educators training clinicians in mind-body approaches
- Health service managers making decisions about offering suggestion-based therapies

title: Delusions are Related to Aberrant Causal Generalization
authors: Martin Meyer, Santiago Castiello de Obeso, Samuel Paskewitz, Philip R. Corlett
summary:
This article explores how aberrant causal generalization tendencies may simultaneously explain the emergence and persistence of delusional beliefs. The authors propose a computational framework based on causal learning and inference models, arguing that delusional phenomenology can be understood as the product of two opposing failures of generalization. On one extreme, individuals who under-generalize information from their experiences may produce "overfitted" models of the world — models that are highly specific to idiosyncratic experiences and therefore generate beliefs that poorly predict or explain social reality. On the other extreme, individuals who over-generalize unusual experiences may develop broadly applied, implausible beliefs that extend far beyond the scope of the original triggering experience.

The authors use computational simulations of learning under different generalization tendencies to demonstrate how these processes can give rise to belief patterns that resemble the phenomenology observed in clinical delusions. By systematically varying parameters governing how broadly or narrowly experiences are generalized during causal learning, the models generate outputs that parallel key features of delusional thinking — including the formation of idiosyncratic beliefs, the resistance of such beliefs to contradictory evidence, and the way in which delusional beliefs can become broadly applied across contexts. The computational approach provides a formal, mechanistic account that bridges the gap between descriptive psychopathology and process-level understanding.

The framework is situated within the broader computational psychiatry literature on belief updating, predictive processing, and hierarchical Bayesian inference. The authors relate aberrant generalization to existing accounts of delusion formation, including prediction error-based models and accounts emphasizing aberrant salience. A key contribution is the bidirectional nature of the proposal: both under-generalization and over-generalization can produce delusional-like beliefs, but through different computational pathways, suggesting that what clinicians may recognise as a single category ("delusions") may in fact arise from distinct mechanistic failures that could benefit from different intervention approaches.
clinical_insight:
This computational framework offers clinicians a novel way of understanding why delusional beliefs can be so resistant to evidence — if the underlying problem lies in how broadly or narrowly a patient generalizes from experience, then simply presenting contradictory facts may not address the computational process driving belief fixation. The distinction between over-generalization and under-generalization as two routes to delusion formation could have implications for treatment personalization: patients whose delusions reflect over-generalization of unusual experiences may benefit from interventions that help them constrain their inferences to appropriate evidence bases, while those with under-generalized models may need support in recognizing broader patterns.

For clinicians working with psychosis, this framework reinforces the importance of understanding the specific reasoning style of each patient rather than treating all delusions as equivalent phenomena. It also provides a theoretical bridge between cognitive-behavioural approaches to delusions and computational neuroscience, potentially informing the development of more targeted cognitive interventions.
relevant_for:
- Researchers in computational psychiatry and delusion modelling
- Clinical psychologists and psychiatrists treating psychotic disorders
- Cognitive neuroscientists studying belief updating and causal learning
- CBT for psychosis practitioners interested in mechanistic accounts of delusions
- Those developing precision psychiatry approaches to schizophrenia spectrum disorders

title: Meditative Development and Psychological Adverse Effects, Challenging, Unpleasant, and Negative Experiences: A Phenomenological Study of Advanced Meditators
authors: Sebastian Ehmann, Terje Sparby, Vanessa Naughton, Andrea Grabovac, Matthew Sacchet
summary:
This qualitative phenomenological study investigated meditation-related psychological adverse effects, challenging, unpleasant, and negative experiences (MRPAEs) in a sample of 28 advanced meditators with extensive long-term practice histories and proficiency in advanced practices. Through 55 semi-structured in-depth interviews conducted remotely, the researchers employed inductive, content-driven thematic analysis with iterative monitoring of theoretical saturation and interrater reliability assessment throughout coding. Methodological reporting adhered to the Consolidated Criteria for Reporting Qualitative Research (COREQ).

Participants described a wide array of negative and appraisal-dependent experiences spanning affective, cognitive, relational, clinical, and existential domains. Seven major themes emerged from the analysis: (1) broadly negative cognitive, attentional, and existential disruptions; (2) negative affect including mood disturbance, anxiety, and fear; (3) adverse impacts on life trajectory and interpersonal relationships; (4) negative clinical and non-clinical transformative experiences, including clinically significant psychiatric symptoms and trauma activation; (5) foundational instability associated with specific meditative insights; (6) ambiguous experiences of context-dependent valence; and (7) resolution strategies including meditative techniques, teacher guidance, and interpersonal support.

The findings demonstrate that advanced meditation can precipitate significant psychological challenges across multiple life domains. While the authors acknowledge that meditative development is also associated with profound positive transformation, they argue that the potential for adverse effects necessitates informed consent, adequate teacher training, and clinical awareness. The breadth and severity of reported experiences challenge the prevailing narrative that meditation is uniformly beneficial and highlight the need for a more nuanced understanding of contemplative practice.
clinical_insight:
This study provides critical evidence that meditation-based interventions, increasingly deployed in clinical settings, carry genuine psychological risks that are underrecognized in both research and practice. Clinicians who recommend or integrate mindfulness and meditation techniques should be aware that even highly experienced practitioners can encounter clinically significant psychiatric symptoms, trauma activation, existential distress, and relationship disruption. The finding that some adverse experiences are appraisal-dependent suggests that clinical framing and preparation may modulate risk.

The identification of resolution strategies—meditative techniques, teacher guidance, and interpersonal support—offers a初步 framework for clinicians encountering meditation-related distress in patients. Clinicians should not assume that meditation-related difficulties are simply a sign of poor practice or lack of skill, given that these effects occurred in advanced practitioners with extensive histories.
relevant_for:
- Clinicians integrating mindfulness-based interventions (MBCT, MBSR, ACT)
- Meditation teachers and retreat facilitators
- Researchers studying contemplative practices and their adverse effects
- Clinical training programs incorporating meditation components
- Patients with trauma histories considering meditation-based therapies

title: Momentary purpose and cognitive function in everyday life: Evidence from the Purpose and Engagement in Everyday Life study
authors: Angelina Sutin, Martina Luchetti, Hannah Lovett, Justin Brown, Antonio Terracciano
summary:
This study leveraged ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data from the Purpose and Engagement in Everyday Life (PEEL) study to examine how momentary fluctuations in purpose in life relate to cognitive performance in daily life. The sample included 2,577 participants (mean age 63.51, SD 11.24; 60% female; 27% Black) who completed cognitive tasks and self-reports three times daily for eight days, yielding over 48,000 assessments with 79% compliance. At each assessment, participants rated momentary purpose, meaning, happiness, and sadness, and completed three cognitive tasks measuring attention, executive function, and visual memory, while also reporting cognitive lapses.

Multilevel modeling revealed that when participants felt more purposeful than their own average, they performed significantly faster on attention (b = -.06, SE = .01, p < .001) and executive function (b = -.11, SE = .04, p = .011) tasks, though no association was found for visual memory. Momentary meaning was associated with attention performance but not executive function or visual memory.
